[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I type this, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. This week's classic of Western occultism is The Book of Druidry by Ross Nichols, founder of the Order of Bards Ovates and Druids and arguably the 20th century's most significant Druid theoretician and teacher. (It's basically a runoff between him and Lewis Spence, several of whose books will feature here in due time.) Nichols was a poet and a painter, and his book is an intensely personal vision of the Druid Revival tradition; as with most of the wortwhile books in that tradition, it's not something to take on faith, but rather well suited to read, brood over, meditate on, and then decide what in it works for you. If you're anywhere in the vast and quirky forest of Revival Druidry, and you haven't read The Book of Druidry yet, I highly recommend it. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you.  See you next week!***
 
[IMAGE:1]

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, I'm quite sure I have Aspergers syndrome, if that's what you're asking. A lot of people don't understand what that amounts to; while there's a lot of variation from person to person, one thing Aspies have in common is that they lack mirror neurons -- the specific net of neurons in a normal human brain that have the function of "mirroring" what goes on in other people's brains, using nonverbal cues. I don't have those, and so I'm very poor at figuring out what other people are thinking and feeling. That's not a lack of emotional intelligence, just the absence of a sense organ most people have -- and the way around it, if you're a novelist, is to pay very close attention to your own inner life, and to read books by other people who reflect on their inner lives (everything from autobiographies and memoirs to good novels).

2) I'd say about half my writing time is writing, the rest is editing. I go over a draft dozens of times, making many small changes and the occasional large one.

3) No and no. Writing is a delight to me. Fiction, especially -- there are very few things in the world I enjoy more than working on a novel when the story's really flowing.

4) We talk over my commentariat fairly often. Sometimes it's comments that are entertaining or insightful, but we also laugh at my trolls together.

comments.title: Druid Grade Consecrations
comments.content:
I consecrated my serpent's egg the other day, and I'm wondering if it worked or not.

A few days before this, I consecrated my sickle. The ritual went smoothly and felt very powerful, as though I had stepped into another world, and all day afterward I felt charged, energized and excited. 

The egg, on the other hand, did not go so well. I've been using a space in the back yard for these rituals, and it happened that during the time I had set aside, one neighbor was doing laundry, which fills the entire neighborhood with the toxic reek of dryer sheets, while the other neighbor was having a pool party! Neither could see me over the fence, but I could hear and smell them.

Noticing this, I did a geomancy chart to determine whether or not the ritual would work. I got: RW: Llosgwrn y Ddraig, LW: Gwyn, J: Bendith Fach. The way of points leads to Llawenydd as the third mother. So I took that as a Yes, and went ahead with it. I assumed that the difficulties with the neighborhood was a test: Do you have the internal power to block out all external distractions and focus on your magic? Then you can do this ritual.

Well, things quickly started to go wrong. The wind blew out my candles. That's fine, it blew out the candles during the sickle consecration, and that went well anyway. But then it blew out my incense. Then my lighter stopped working, so I had to pause the ritual, go inside, light the incense on the stove, and come back out. When I got to the SRP, I accidentally made the sign of Earth while calling Fire, didn't realize it until I was calling Water, and decided to start the ritual again from the beginning. When I got to the egg itself, I felt that I didn't hold it for long enough in the Earth pentagram, and so I wasn't sure if it got enough Earth energy. I messed up a sentence of the analysis of the Grand Word, which wouldn't have been that big a deal (I re-read the ritual earlier on in the day and found I'd been saying part of it incorrectly), but it was when added on top of everything else.

Finally, once the ritual was done I felt drained, disappointed, and somewhat depressed. After consecrating my sickle, I felt like I had just come back from a visit to the kingdom of Logres; the only issue was that the mundane world felt rather dull for a day or so. After the egg, it was the opposite-- I felt like I'd spent the hour badly, and I wanted to be back in the regular world. 

Some parts of the ritual did feel powerful. I noticed when I consecrated my wand that, when I held it in the pentagrams, it felt like I was extending it into the elemental realms, for all the beings there to behold; I had the same feeling to at least some extent this time. The grand word felt powerful, even though I made a mistake, and so did the Spirit pentagram, although I wasn't sure whether I should use the same or opposite-gender deity for the name of the 9th sphere. (And actually I can't remember which one I used.)

I also noticed, though, that my attitude toward the tool in question was very different between the sickle and the egg. After consecrating the sickle, I felt like swinging it around like a mighty weapon. The egg, on the other hand, felt way too heavy and awkward as I wore it during the various banishings. 

Given all that, though, do you think it was a failed consecration? Should I repeat the consecration, or should I treat it as a mildly-flubbed initiation (like Regardie's adeptus minor initiation?) that can nevertheless have an enduring effect?

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content: This is a common experience, and it doesn't mean that the consecration failed. You might choose to repeat the consecration, but if you do that, reconsecrate all of your working tools, and think of it as a way of adding power to things already powerful, and of getting more experience with rituals of this kind.

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content: Thank you, that's a relief! If it isn't a consecration failure, what IS going on then when this happens?

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content: Very often it's a form of testing. You have to show that you can push through apparent disappointment in order to reach the next level of empowerment.

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content: Okay that makes sense, and sounds like exactly the sort of thing I've come to expect from the Whoever They Are that run the show behind the scenes. Thank you again.

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content:
So you could consecrate them a dozen times if you wanted? I imagine there's a point of diminishing returns where you'd stop noticing any difference in power after consecrating them?

(Not the OP btw)

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content: Yes, you can consecrate them once a month for the rest of your life if you want to. It would be very interesting to see if there is in fact a point of diminishing returns.

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content: If doing the ritual component of a consecration poorly doesn't necessarily mean the consecration failed, then what does typically cause a consecration to fail and how would one know whether a consecration worked or not?

replies.title: Re: Druid Grade Consecrations
replies.content:
Failed consecrations are rare. You'd have to leave out a crucial element of the ritual, or do the whole thing without any inner work at all. Far more common are weak consecrations, and that can be made good readily enough by repeating the consecration.

As for knowing how well the consecration worked, that usually takes some degree of clairvoyant ability.

comments.title: Evil eye
comments.content: What's your take on the 'evil eye'? I can't find any good information beyond 'folklore' that it's a real effect, and the forelore is quite contradictory. Some seem to say it's a deliberate malicious intent passed on by a stare, others that it's an accidental gaze causing misfortune, even without any intent or knowledge. Blue eyes seem to be particularly implicated, but I don't know if this just due to the relative scarcity of blue eyes in the Mediterranean and middle Eastern regions where the belief is still prevalent? Does this relate to the Platonic belief in 'eye-beams' as well, or was that just a flawed theory of optics? I know from experience that I can definitely tell when I'm being watched, and most pets seem to hate cameras. So do eyes, and other image-collecting devices like cameras, emit as well as receive? If so, does anything screen this emission out? (eg. sunglasses)

replies.title: Re: Evil eye
replies.content:
Plato's "eye beams" were, like a lot of early Greek physics, based on a confusion between the material plane and the subtle planes. There are no beams of physical energy flowing out through the eyes, but there are beams of subtle energy projected from the eyes -- you might have had the experience of a lover's glance feeling like a caress; on the etheric and astral planes, it is one. The evil eye is the flip side of that. It can be done intentionally by someone who knows how, but you also get people who are simply so full of malice that it oozes from them via every available outlet. The traditional protections against the Eye are useful if you have to deal with such a person.

As for animals and cameras, my guess there is that it's because cameras don't project anything etheric or astral. They look like eyes, but they're dead, and so animals react to them the way you'd react to being stared at by a corpse.

replies.title: Re: Evil eye
replies.content: Are those beams of subtle energy that come from the eyes the reason that some people can sense when someone else is looking at them?

replies.title: Re: Evil eye
replies.content: Yes, exactly.

replies.title: Re: Evil eye
replies.content:
Interesting, thanks. Do some materials such as glass block a certain proportion of the subtle energy (in the same way that glass blocks most UV light), and that's why it's harder to tell if you're being watched from behind glass? (Hence interrogation rooms with their silvered glass, and secret service agents with their mirror shades)

On a related note, I just remembered an experiment I read about some years ago where two populations of bacteria taken from the same original stock (ie. they were therefore more or less genetically identical) were placed next to each other, with a glass sheet between them. The bacteria in one dish were poisoned, and died. The other population were, as you would expect, unharmed.

The experiment was then repeated with two more sets of bacteria (again from the same original stock), but with a sheet of quartz glass between them. This time though, when the first set of bacteria were poisoned, the second set sickened and died fairly soon after. Quartz glass is far more transparent to UV light than silica glass, so I imagine it might let other energies through more easily as well. I don't recall what weasel words the scientists came up with to explain their results though! I'll try to find the details of the study again if I can.

replies.title: military chaplains
replies.content:
The Graduate Theological Union in Berkeley, California is an old established consortium of a number of different postgrad religious schools (the only one I can name off hand is Starr King, a Unitarian-Universalist school) The GTU has had openly pagan students since the 1980s if not earlier, and some of them have been awarded degrees. It's a solid school; degrees from there would be respected by the military.

Cherry Hill Seminary is a homegrown Pagan institution of higher learning which began awarding Masters Degrees a couple of years ago, but they are unaccredited so not very useful for getting any kind of job.

The required two years of ministerial experience might be hard to document since few pagan religions in the US are congregational, engage in missionary work, or support some other institution that serves enough people to have the need for a minister. The workaround for that might be to do ministerial work for a nonsectarian nonprofit in the area of human services, such as advocacy for the homeless. Something like that would be good preparation for being a military chaplain, because chaplains in the military are supposed to be prepared to minister to all servicepeople, not just members of their own faith.

Assuming a person did all that and aspired to be a military chaplain, if that person were a witch of some kind, he or she could join the Covenant of the Goddess, qualify for ministerial credentials from CoG, and probably get an Ecclesiastical Endorsement from the Covenant without too much difficulty. The Covenant of the Goddess was incorporated in 1975, has chapters in many parts of the USA, and the DoD has heard of it.

comments.title: Invoking the muse

replies.title: Re: Invoking the muse
replies.content: That's a very common experience for writers. Go with it, let it happen, and make room for it -- for example, make a habit of spending some time every day just writing the first thing that comes into your head. Whether it's your unconscious mind or something transpersonal is a very difficult question to answer, but if you treat it as something transpersonal and listen attentively to it, the results tend to be very good.

comments.title: Extended FAQ-Question
comments.content:
Der JMG,
first oft all, thanks for setting up the FAQ-site, site, so I didn't have to ask the pregnant/children question again. Still, there is this: We moved, and as is our custom we leave the blinds in our bedroom open at night. Our downstairs neighbour has (two nights since) installed a lightchain on her balcony and in her garden. It is the blinking variety and the reflection on our ceiling keeps me from sleeping. Of course we closed the blinds, but I got the impression, that the light was installed to harm us. Being of the lining-walls-with-books kind, I have not much magical experience, still I put up a mirror in a certain way, as to reflect back any intended harm. So here the question: my child is a bit over a year, and I'm still nursing, am I harming our child/me?

replies.title: Re: Extended FAQ-Question
replies.content:
Ahem. Here's what the FAQ says:

"What about natural magic? Kids are fine around natural magic—in most traditional cultures it’s common to use various forms of natural magic to protect and bless children from the moment they’re born. If you’re pregnant or nursing, have your partner or a friend do the magical work; otherwise, you can do as you wish. By the way, many children love natural magic and can be introduced to the simpler forms of it at an early age; teaching your kids to make an amulet with herbs of protection and blessing, and recite a prayer over it, makes a great project for a rainy day."

Putting up a mirror is a form of natural magic. It's about the simplest form you can do, so no, it shouldn't be a problem for you. If you decide you want amulets or the like, get your partner to make them.

comments.title: Christo-Druidry
comments.content:
Hello JMG! The Accidental Mystic here. After several weeks of contemplating the Druid gods, and also reading Ross Nichols' book, I came to the conclusion that diving deeper into that current of Revival Druidry probably isn't right for me.

However, my attempts to connect with the Druid gods gave me the tools that I needed to finally make some kind of sense of Jesus Christ… I threw out orthodox theology and approached him as I would any other (pagan) deity. I also found your essays in Ascendant and Jesus Through Pagan Eyes edifying.

I was very surprised, a little embarrassed, and hesitantly pleased to realize that he was a god whom I'd already met and made a connection with! But that connection wasn't made through the Church or Bible, and I hadn't recognized him from his Sunday school depictions. I'd just been calling him 'God', which he obviously answers to...

All of this has led me into an interesting predicament. Is it possible to be, well, essentially a Christ henotheist? Is that a thing that anyone does? That a Druid can do?

Also, if one is worshipping Christ outside of the context of the Church, is it advisable to draw on the traditional prayers and formulas of said Church? Many of them have a, well, a weight to them - a sense of power that my own prayers seem to lack. Those 'tracks in space' that are so often mentioned here, I suppose.

However, another thing that gets mentioned is 'tainted egregores', and I don't particularly want to invite the less savory energies of the Church egregore into my worship inadvertently. Is that a concern?

Finally, can you recommend a book in the western occult tradition that has a really thorough explanation of the life force, under whatever name?

Thanks as always for your time and generosity!

replies.title: Re: Christo-Druidry
replies.content:
Not JMG,

I found Nichols' book too Judeo-Christian for my taste. It might be due to his affiliation with Martinism. I recommend 'The Mysteries of Britain' by Lewis Spence as a good source for Druid Revival tradition.

replies.title: Re: Christo-Druidry
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. Probably half the Druids in the Revival tradition have been some form of Christian. There are a lot of Christian henotheists around these days, too, in and out of Druidry, so you're on familiar ground.

As for the formulae of "the Church," a vast amount depends on which church you have in mind. I don't recommend using any of the formulas specifically linked to the Roman Catholic church unless you want to be drawn into its egregor. On the other hand, pan-Christian prayers and formulae such as the Lord's Prayer and the Apostle's Creed have been being used by Christian occultists for a very long time with good results. You might see if you can find a copy of Peter Roche de Coppens' The Nature and Use of Ritual, which provides detailed information on the esoteric dimension of these formulae.

Finally, I wish there was a good book on the life force from a Western occult perspective. I don't know of one.

comments.title: Memory Lapses
comments.content: What can one do to make oneself remember to carry out minor household duties? Nothing difficult, just self-assigned, basic things like put the butter back in the fridge overnight, or make sure the screen door is latched every night. Part of the problem is lack of a regular routine and dislike of routine in general. But punitive measures like withholding privileges or pleasures for infractions of the Rule are not working. Frex, no sweets for a week or no TV movie with dinner for a week for each infraction. That works for a while but then relapses happen. Just plain willing does not work. Affirmations have not worked.  What does work, over the long haul? Putting up post-it notes on every available surface? Prayer to appropriate deities? This is not senility, just chronic inattention or unresolved teenage rebellion or something...?

BTW, what you've described trying sounds very much like a parent trying to discipline a small child, without trying to understand the child's point of view. replies.title: Re: Memory Lapses
replies.content:
Since asking the question, and receiving answers from friends and fellows here in this forum, I have been thinking about the issue and coming to some tentative realizations.

– Tradeoffs of attention, like other forms of energy and awareness, can be had, but they come at a stiff price. Gaining more outer world awareness means suffering less inner world absorption and the discomfort of operating outside one’s habitual zone. Less fantasy fiction; more real time dusting and floor swabbing. Bo-o-o-ring.
– Early bent to the character, partly natal tendencies often reinforced through years of ‘tracks’ and habits, and just now enduring Opposition from some heavy duty planets to natal aspects.
– Reaction resistence to some of the knowledge gained here and the points of view JMG argues all too eloquently for that kind of blew away previous notions about reality and opened a dauntingly complex conception of how the world actually works which has undermined long-held world-views, life goals, ideals, and sense of self.
– A regressive reluctance to grow up and face up to this new wideness of world that does not appear as an exciting prospect, or not exciting in a good way. Just more to fear and more to endure and far less to enjoy. A dreary round of dull duty, tedious obligations, exacting and unrewarding labor. Very little can be accomplished at all; and whatever ‘road less travelled’ tracks one lays down in space are all too apt to be obliterated by the deeper tracks laid down by the thundering herd of common humanity, hoofing along.
– And in any case kindergarten just keeps going on and on. The prospect of enduring thousands upon thousands of painful, ugly lives in order to micro-inch towards a ‘graduate’ level of being that merely entails still more responsibility and danger and hard work does not appeal. It seems to give something to others and bully for them. Yay. Rah. Not so much in this corner, but hey it’s a very narrow place, and easy to overlook in the Grand Growth Process.
– A mute, sullen, hopeless protest against loss of so much meaning and purpose, together with contracting and dissolving community connections and the unpleasant surprises that arrive with an aging body. A retreat into absentia as a self-defeating resistence to being regulated to the point of losing freedom. A child’s idea of what freedom is or can or ought to be.

So yeah, no wonder affirmations do not take a firm hold. A lot of weight to shift there.

Don’t think I am replicating the parental units. When it comes to dusting and housekeeping am in full riotous and gleeful rebellion against that. But these are things *I* want me to remember to do. Feels more like a snarky selfish inner teen than a small child. But I can take another look.

Thanks for the several practical ideas, fellow readers. Will try them on for size and fit.

replies.title: Re: Memory Lapses
replies.content: Maybe try rewarding yourself for household duties accomplished instead of punishing yourself for forgetting.  Also, thank the item for its service, for instance, thank the door for keeping you safe and secure before you lock it for the night.  Similarly, thank the butter and the butter dish for their hard work.  If you accidentally leave the butter dish out, don't make a big deal of it, just say, "I'm sorry I forgot you last night, thanks for your patience" and put the butter dish back in the fridge.  Praying to Mercury doesn't seem like a bad idea either.

replies.title: Re: Memory Lapses
replies.content:
As for negative consequences and positive reinforcement, I thought it was just part of parenting to use both together. To carry out a very few required tasks before bedtime is not a harsh thing to ask of any child or inner teen. Keeping food fresh and the doors secure at night are responsibilites that benefit health, hearth, and safety. There is no good excuse for ‘forgetting’ to fulfill such basic duties. Inside or outside the home, failure to carry out assigned tasks is going to have negative consequences, which I impose on myself as a matter of just being a grown-up.

But the resentful childish/teenish inner selves have a means of mitigation, if they choose. The deal is that whatever negative consequence is in force, its duration can be whittled down gradually by doing extra chores. That is, the sentence is abated by one whole day for doing two hours per day of “household service” (like a community service sentence). This requires a mere fraction of the day spent in tidying the living area, sorting out stacks of paperwork, or cleaning the floors in the craft area. These chores need doing, but seldom get done. Thus, a win-win: the sentence is shortened in total duration, or abated for the day; and the household space is improved. But it is not working, lately. Too much resistance. Churlish are the low in spirit.

It is not the house, the deities or the food dishes that are offended against; it is me: my own self. My will is weakened by failure to keep my word; my honor is defiled by heedless neglect of my responsibilities. How does one properly address Mercury/Hermes? I rather dislike the color orange—maybe I should put up orange post-its as homage to Hermes until my sub-unconcious selves decide to cooperate?

replies.title: Re: Divination, Religious Syncretism
replies.content:
My own familiarity with Afro-Diasporic religions is limited to some reading, receiving a divination from a white Santero, and participating in a few Umbanda rituals and a few Santeria rituals which were led by and attended by white people. Having said that, I take a different tack in my interpretation of the requirement.

The requirement that initiates be baptized RC might have begun for practical reasons, either to avoid persecution, or to be sure that all initiates have a common stock of experiences and compatible world views so that antagonists won't have to be weeded out of the community after they have been given the keys to get in. It may be that Roman Catholicism and the African religions that are sources for Candomble have complementary strengths. No religion is equally good at everything.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Was the early Gnosticism a part of Christianity, or was it the other way around?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nothing so straightforward. In the two centuries or so to either side of the beginning of the Common Era, there was a whole gallimaufry of different religious movements all heading in roughly toward the same territory that Christianity finally occupied: monotheistic, ascetic, scriptural, and focused on leaving behind the sinful world for a perfect otherworld on the far side of death. Some of those movements used to word "Gnosis" a lot, and so the label "Gnostic" came into general use. Christianity was born in that context and drew heavily from it, especially via Paul. Later on, as doctrines hardened and hierarchies became strong, there was a major push to get rid of a lot of the Gnostic and quasi-Gnostic material; if the Gospel of Thomas is as old as it looks, this involved quietly suppressing some things that had been part of Christianity from the beginning. It was only at that point, though, that it became possible to talk of Christianity and Gnosticism as two definitely different things.

replies.title: (no subject)

Can you share any insights as to why the current derangement runs so deep?

Example 1: I saw an article in IEEE Spectrum (a magazine for EEs) showing that US oil production didn't tail off as Hubbert predicted, granting that the recent (until very recent) surge was due to extraordinary effort, and thus asserting that we'll always have the oil we need (because we'll always think of some new extraordinary effort). Example 2: The HCQ (hydroxychloroquine) anti-malarial medication got a top-page editorial in the Washington Post, saying "forget about it!". They cite two observational studies which observed that, if you wait until patients are within a day or two of ICU admission, HCQ doesn't rescue them. (Never mind that people with ongoing HCQ prescriptions for pre-existing illness seem largely immune to COVID-19.) By the time a person reaches the ER, a patient has probably enough damage that stopping the virus would be too late. Of course, no vaccine could rescue them at this point, either!

Example 3: fabric face covers. "They might even do more harm than good!" the headline screams. But in the Czech Republic, the population mobilized to provide home-sewn masks for everyone, and promoted the slogan "my mask protects you; your mask protects me". As of today, the country logged 8527 cases, with just 298 deaths. OK, it's population is about half that of New York state, but NY had 28,000 deaths (almost 100x). With so much uncertainty about the facts of each example, what happened to open-minded patience for new developments? replies.title: Re: The gods and activism
replies.content:
I concur on the identification of the Morrigan as a symbol of female empowerment. Early Second Wave feminism engaged in critiques of every aspect of culture and society. When some feminists turned their attention to religion, they noticed that the images and words used about deity in the Abrahamic religions are overwhelmingly masculine, that women were excluded from positions of leadership and authority, and that these two practices were a self-reinforcing feedback loop.

Feminists who did not want to reject religion altogether looked at goddesses in polytheistic religions for alternative role models. Naturally, some were inspired by the most fearsome aspects of female divinity. Those who hadn't been born at that time might not know that during the middle of the twentieth century there was little attention or respect given to women's sports, women were generally believed to be inherently physically weak and incapable of doing much, heroines in popular TV and movies usually had no roles other than as love interests and rescue objects, and the idea that women could serve in combat roles in the military was radical and not taken seriously.

Under those cultural conditions, women who did not want to be weak or to be treated as lesser beings were often attracted to the most bad-ass goddesses they could find. Artemis and Kali were early favorites. The Morrigan, being less well known, acquired a following later.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
According to the Druid tradition, yes, you can flunk out of humanity and have to go back and do some more lives as animals. Welsh people with the second sight traditionally can see the cydfil or future animal-shape of an evildoer pacing alongside the person; George Macdonald, who knew a lot more than he let on, put that into his children's novel The Princess and Curdie.

At least in theory, though, the human level of being is where that kind of testing takes place. Before that point, you don't have enough free will to mess yourself over -- instinct, which is the voice of the guardian spirit of each species, keeps you more or less on track. Once you pass the test of humanity and rise up into the levels beyond, you don't have to worry about falling back. It's our level of being where the testing happens, and you can keep coming back as many times as you need to, until you get it right.

comments.title: plant alchemy
comments.content:
In your Natural Magic book you specify 175 proof vodka or rum for the alchemical preparation of herbs. The highest proof available in California is 151. Will this work? If so does the process need to be adjusted in any way?

Rita

replies.title: Re: plant alchemy
replies.content: Not sure if the alchemical preparation in question is destined to be a tincture or not but if it is 151 works just as well. You can make herbal tinctures with 80 proof if that's all you can get your hands on.

replies.title: Re: plant alchemy
replies.content:
No, this isn't for tinctures--its the process of grinding the plant material, then soaking it in alcohol, then screening out the original material, burning off the residue of alcohol, calcining the dry material and then gradually recombining with the alcohol extract. I assume the high proof is necessary for the alcohol to burn off. I can get 100 proof vodka without difficulty and 151 rum.

As you may or may not know, the US has a patchwork of alcohol regulations on the state (or in some states, such as Texas, even on the county level) and overall taxation and regulation by the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives. I add this info because I have observed that many of JMG's readers are outside the US and can be confused by the oddities of our system.

Rita

replies.title: Re: plant alchemy
replies.content: You might want to sneak over the border into Oregon or Nevada and pick up a few bottles of Everclear, which is 190 proof and makes a fine alchemical menstruum. If you have to use 151 proof, let it macerate an extra few weeks.

replies.title: Re: plant alchemy
replies.content: Everclear is available in California. I got some for use as a disinfectant.

Aleister Crowley's inner plane contact (Aiwass) looks like an alien. Was the pop-culture depiction of aliens influenced by Thelemites' description, or were those alleged "aliens" something else?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's an interesting point. Those aliens we all think of, the ones with the bulbous heads and big dark eyes, were invented by Hollywood. They first appeared in the made-for-TV movie The UFO Incident, which was a fictionalized version of the famous Betty and Barney Hill abduction:
[IMAGE:0]
The fascinating thing is that that wasn't anything like what the Hills described. Betty Hill said that the aliens that abducted her had black hair and big noses like actor Jimmy Durante; Barney described them as "Nazis." (There were a lot of places where their accounts diverged.) Some Hollywood makeup artist came up with something that fit the pop-culture notion of an alien better, and the Grays were born. Of course the image went through further refinement at the hands of Whitley Streiber et al. Did any of this have anything to do with Aiwass, or Lam, or any of Crowley's other looks-like-an-alien images? I don't think anybody knows.

comments.title: Saturn and the Changer
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer:

Thank you as always for these weekly offerings of wisdom and discourse. Truly brightens one's Mondays! As for my questions:

1. You have mentioned a few times on various blogs that you never do curses due to the karmic consequences and the "rasperry jam" principle, and the most aggressive/malefic magic you'll do is a "Saturnian Binding." Can you expand on that? I've found a lot of conflicting information online, and so my subset-questions are:

a) What makes a Saturnian Binding less destructive in its consequences than a straight-out curse? Is it that you're asking the planetary energies to do the work, rather than running through you? Is it because Saturn is traditionally associated with Justice and boundaries, and the karmic consequences are simply that any justice or karma you have coming towards you will have a bit of an "express delivery"?

b) What traditions are Saturnian Bindings from? If one wanted to learn how do do something like that (without it all blowing up in one's face) how would one go about that?

c) Does a Saturnian Binding have a higher purpose? As in, if it is meant to restore justice, and be an "express karma delivery", does it not help you clear up/force you to deal with your own karma as well? And would that not help you, ultimately, as it would mean you would face your next incarnation with less baggage?

2: You have mentioned several times now that the archetype of "The Changer" is on his way towards a thorough reshaping of American life and politics. What, if anything, can we do to get a better sense of what that might look like? Furthermore:

a) the Native American cultures that we displaced were, for the most part, extremely conservative societies. Given that they are possibly a tolerable rubric for understanding what human cultures would be like after multiple encounters with the Changer, can this be understood as a possible reason for why he seems to be favoring the right, rather than the left?

b) They were also rather fragmented, with rather few continent-spanning empires to be found. Given that the Changer has made that fragmentation less likely, rather than more, what do you think is going on there? Is that simply delayed? Or if, as you say, we might "pull off a Byzantium", would that somehow also be accompanied by political fragmentation, as Byzantium itself occupied significantly less territory than the old Roman Empire?

Thank you also for your patience with my long-winded questions! Any help or insight on any of these matters is greatly appreciated. Will be tipping shortly!

replies.title: Re: Saturn and the Changer
replies.content:
1a) No, you've misunderstood the point of a binding. A binding simply stops somebody from doing something. It doesn't bring any negative consequences down on the person: it just interferes with their capacity to, say, harm a person you want to protect. Nor are all bindings Saturnine; I've also invoked Mars very effectively in some cases.

1b) A good thorough grounding in Golden Dawn magic will give you the background to know what to do. This isn't something for beginners or amateurs.

1c) Again, you've misunderstood the point of a binding spell; see my comment to 1a. A bindign spell isn't a curse, it doesn't bring down karma, or anything like that. It just stops someone from doing something. The blowback is that you won't be able to do it either. Since the bindings I've done were to stop or prevent such things as physical abuse and rape, I have no problem that that.

2) Try to get a sense of what differentiates American culture from its current European pseudomorphosis; that should help.

2a) That's a massive overgeneralization. Some Native American societies were conservative, others were highly innovative. At the moment, the Democratic party is the main conservative force in American public life -- it's trying to maintain the status quo -- while the populist movement that's taken over the GOP is pushing for change. The Changer seems to want those changes to happen.

2b) Continent-spanning empires are rare in any corner of human history. The Native American cultures were not especially fragmented by comparison -- the Hodenosaunee (Iroquois) Confederacy was rapidly evolving into a regional power the size of a large European nation, for example, at the time Columbus arrived. As for the Changer's intervention, how do you see that as involving less fragmentation? It's the affluent-corporate Left that's pushing for rigid cultural uniformity, while the populist movement seeks more regional autonomy.

replies.title: Re: Saturn and the Changer
replies.content: Thanks for all of these, this has given me a lot to think about.

Hello Mr. Greer,

I've heard a claim that Jamal al-Din Afghani was the real person behind some "secret chief" figures like "Serapis Bey" (Blavatsky's tutor) and "Haji Sharif" (Alveydre's tutor). Is that true?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have no idea. It's an interesting speculation, though.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Kenneth Paul Johnson did a great deal of solid scholarly hard work on which historic persons might have been concealed by Blavatsky under her various hidden masters. See his "In Search of the Masters" (1990) and "The Masters Revealed" (1994), if you can find them; they are long lout of print and rare. Jamal al-din Afghani (IIRC) is one of the most influential of them; I don't remember which psuedonym Blavatsky used for him. I think it likely that al-Afghani also had dealings with another of the founders of the short-lived original Theosophical Society in New York, Emma Hardinge Britten, and is reflected in some parts of her fascinating occult works "Ghost Land" and "Art Magic" (both 1876).

(Historical note for any commentors who are also Theosophists. It is provably false that Blavatsky and Olcott were the two principal founders of the TDS in its New York period. They were indeed the two (re)-founders of the revived TS in India, but they coopted its earlier history into their own mythology of how the TS came about.)
